entries.title: ‘Turning Up the Heat on the Streets’
entries.summary: The problem with ICE and traffic stops
entries.content:
Subscribe here: Apple Podcasts | Spotify | YouTube | Overcast | Pocket Casts

Friday, Department of Homeland Security Secretary Markwayne Mullin was asked at a press conference about his previous comments of “turning down the temperature.” This was a reasonable question given that two men, one in Texas and one in Maine, were recently shot and killed by immigration agents who approached them in their cars. A few days earlier, following conversations with Republican Senator Susan Collins of Maine, Mullin had said he was temporarily suspending traffic stops, which certainly sounded like a temperature turndown. But then Donald Trump promptly stomped on that idea: “We CANNOT give up on I.C.E.’s most important and effective Crime Fighting tools, THE TRAFFIC STOP!” the president wrote on Truth Social. So in response to the question about temperature Friday, Mullin gave the answer his boss required: “I turn down the temperature with you guys,” he said, pointing to the reporters, and continued, “we’re turning up the heat on the streets.”

Right now immigration enforcement is in a combustible place. Both of the recent killings involved traffic stops, which immigration officers have less experience and training in than other law enforcement. The Maine killing involved a new recruit who, according to emerging reports, had a history of violent behavior and serious mental-health issues. Since the killing of two American citizens in Minnesota in January, approval for Trump’s immigration-enforcement tactics has dipped. And yet he is getting pressure from his supporters to stay firm. Despite the recent killings, the administration says it is still trying for 2,000 immigration arrests a day.

Our guest today is the staff writer Nick Miroff, who covers immigration. We talk about the details of the shootings, the problem with the traffic stop, and the direction Mullin seems to be taking the agency.

The following is a transcript of the episode:

Nick Miroff: These ICE officers are swooping in out of nowhere, in some cases trying to pin the vehicle of the person that they want to arrest. And, you know, if they do this hundreds of times a day across the country in multiple cities, it stands to reason that in some cases the people they’re after are going to panic. And if they panic and try to drive away, does that, you know, justify killing them, you know? And the answer is no, of course, but it points to the inherent risks of these kinds of tactics by ICE officers.

Hanna Rosin: The recent killings of two men—Lorenzo Salgado Araujo in Texas and Joan Sebastián Durán Guerrero in Maine—by immigration officers have put the Department of Homeland Security back in the place that Secretary Markwayne Mullin was trying to avoid.

Markwayne Mullin (from his Senate confirmation hearing on March 18): My goal, in six months, is that we’re not in the lead story every single day.

So, the administration didn’t want to be on the front page, but it didn’t want to give up on its aggressive immigration agenda either. Recently it’s ramped up immigration arrests to 2,000 a day. And Friday Mullin said they are staying the course.

Mullin (from his press conference on July 17): We’re turning up the heat on the streets. We’re out there working harder than we ever have because we’ve empowered our law enforcement to do their job.

Many of the recent arrests were traffic stops—that’s how the men in Texas and Maine were killed. And in Maine, the officer involved was a new recruit with, reports say, a history of violent behavior and serious mental-health issues.

So they are definitely back in the headlines.

I’m Hanna Rosin. This is Radio Atlantic. Joining me today to talk about the two recent killings and about DHS Secretary Mullin, is Nick Miroff, who covers immigration for The Atlantic.

Rosin: Hey Nick. Welcome to the show.

Miroff: Thanks. Good to be with you.

Rosin: So after Renee Good and Alex Pretti were shot and killed in Minnesota, there was a lot of pushback against the way immigration enforcement was being done, including by Republicans, including by some MAGA people. So how did we get back here again with two killings by immigration officers in such a short period?

Miroff: Yeah, well, there was a kind of a broader pullback, right? I mean, the administration sent Tom Homan into Minneapolis to clean things up. The president ousted Kristi Noem. Markwayne Mullin came in as the new DHS secretary, signaling that he wanted things to be quieter, to keep DHS out of the headlines. And yet the calls on the right started, you know, throughout the spring were growing for them to intensify their ICE operations. People were seeing the ICE arrests and deportation numbers lower than they had been prior to Minneapolis. And Markwayne Mullin as well as “border czar” Tom Homan were getting accused on the right of backing off of Trump’s promises to carry out the largest, you know, deportation campaign in U.S. history.

And so what we’ve seen in the last few weeks was a rapid intensification of ICE activity. They have been arresting about twice as many people as they were doing a few months ago. And they were trying to do it more quietly. It didn’t look like Minneapolis, because it wasn’t all concentrated in one city. It wasn’t Border Patrol agents, you know, sweeping through neighborhoods. It was the kind of targeted enforcement that Tom Homan signaled that he wanted, to double down on the kind of tactics that ICE has always done.

But this time it’s been more focused on vehicle stops, not on homes. It’s on vehicle stops. And these vehicle stops are inherently dangerous for many of the reasons that we have just seen.

Rosin: What do we know about the details of these shootings so far? Because we haven’t had the kind of videos emerge like we did in Minnesota, where we could see clearly what happened at the moment of shooting.

Miroff: You know, I’d say one thing else about that, which is, you know, if you think about Minneapolis, because every everyone was so stirred up and the protesters were out in the streets, it was almost impossible for ICE to carry out some kind of arrest like this without somebody filming it on a cellphone. Says a lot about how much more quietly they’ve been able to operate since Minneapolis. And the focus on the body cams, you know, is once again become a contentious matter.

ICE got, you know, tens of billions of dollars obviously last year through the One Big Beautiful Bill [Act] and got another several tens of billions through the reconciliation bill that passed prior to the shutdown. So the White House is saying it’s the Democrats’ fault that the body cameras haven’t been distributed across the country, but Democrats are pointing out that they had tens of billions of dollars at their disposal, you know, last summer that they could have used on body cameras. And Kristi Noem after Minneapolis said that they were gonna get body cameras out to ICE officers around the country. And here we are six months later with no body-camera footage of either fatal shooting.

Rosin: What do we know about each of these incidents? Because it sounds like the traffic-stop element is important. So, so what do we know?

Miroff: Well, I mean what’s, I think, you know, one of the first things that’s significant about both incidents is that the officers killed someone who wasn’t the target of their arrest operation that day.

So in Houston, the team that went out and decided to pull over the work van driven by Lorenzo Salgado Araujo, the 52-year-old construction contractor and father of three U.S. citizens. In that case, they were looking for somebody who they have not named, and they were doing the kind of targeted enforcement that they’ve been doing across the country. Typically they go early in the morning to try to catch people as they’re coming out of their homes and getting into their cars or heading off to work. You know, the tactics that ICE has relied on historically to get people at their homes—that doesn’t work anymore. People no longer open their doors for ICE. They know that officers need a judicial warrant in order to force their way into a home.

And so ICE has kind of given up on that tactic and they’re trying to catch people as they’re leaving for work, and that’s what they were doing with this work van, even though Mr. Salgado was not the target of their operation.

And so what we know is that they tried to pull him over. There’s surveillance-camera footage showing that he didn’t obey their commands, that he continued to drive. His sons have said that they thought he probably panicked. He was worried that he was getting robbed for his work tools that were his livelihood.

So he didn’t stop, and they tried to box him in. The Department of Homeland Security said that he tried to weaponize his vehicle and drive at them. Reporters who went to the scene did not see any major impacts on any of the vehicles suggesting that there was a big crash. And there are eyewitness accounts claiming that the ICE officer who shot Salgado was reaching through the open passenger window, shot him in the stomach, and then the officers pulled him out of the vehicle and tried to handcuff him. There’s a bystander video recorded that shows two officers over him, and he’s moaning in pain. We know he was taken to the hospital after that, where he died.

Rosin: You mentioned that Salgado’s sons said he might have panicked when the police tried to stop him because he thought they were trying to rob him. That’s not what a lot of people would picture as a traffic stop. Like, you would see the police lights flashing; you would know that it was a police car. So, what is a traffic stop like by an immigration-enforcement agency?

Miroff: Yeah, that’s a great question. These ICE vehicle stops are not like traffic stops conducted by normal police officers with marked cars and flashing lights. These are ICE officers, typically in plain clothes driving in unmarked cars. And they’re really relying on the element of surprise. It isn’t like driving past a police car on the highway and then it turns on its lights and you don’t understand why you’re being pulled over. These ICE officers are swooping in out of nowhere, in some cases trying to pin the vehicle of the person that they want to arrest. And, you know, if they do this hundreds of times a day across the country in multiple cities, it stands to reason that in some cases, the people they’re after are going to panic. And if they panic and try to drive away, does that, you know, justify killing them, you know? And the answer is no, of course, but it points to the inherent risks of these kinds of tactics by ICE officers.

Rosin: So that’s Texas. What about the incident in Maine?

Miroff: We saw another targeted enforcement operation gone wrong in the town of Bitterford, Maine. And in that incident, what we know is that a team of officers went to a residence looking for someone who had what’s called a final order of removal; an immigration judge had ordered them to leave the country. But it was not the person that ICE ended up shooting.

And so, a Colombian father named Joan Sebastián Guerrero was on his way to work coming out of his home. Again, the officers claim that he used his vehicle in a way that they described as a threat to public safety. We’ve seen security footage showing the aftermath of the shooting, in which the vehicle is kind of doing loops in an intersection after they have shot him.

The officer involved fired at least four shots through the windshield. He was struck in the head. There’s also footage showing them taking him out of the vehicle and attempting to handcuff him as he’s dying.

And we know that the officer who fired was a new hire—in this case, an officer who had federal law-enforcement experience working for the Veterans Affairs Department Police Force, but had been hired by ICE just this year. And I think, you know, more details are coming out about this officer’s training and his background.

Rosin: Yeah, and those details are pretty alarming. Members of his family told the media that he had a history of mental-health issues dating back to when he was a preteen and also a history of violent behaviors. So that’s that one officer in Maine.

Now more broadly, another thing that’s come up a lot is traffic stops. Both of these killings involved traffic stops. So what’s the significance of that? Is that something that these immigration officers are not particularly trained to handle?

Miroff: Yeah. What I’ve been told is that, you know, ICE officers not only went through this kind of fast-track training program last fall and into this winter, because of the White House’s urgency in getting, you know, thousands of more trainees out onto the streets; that generally ICE officers don’t get as much training in safe vehicle stops as, say, large police departments and that at the same time they are increasingly relying on this tactic in order to make their arrest numbers and to meet the pressure from the White House to have more and more arrests and deportations.

And so they’re relying on this tactic. They don’t have what police departments call a vehicle pursuit policy, that really establishes, like, clear guidelines as to when you can use force during a vehicle stop. Because what the Supreme Court has established is that you cannot shoot somebody just because they’re disobeying your commands or they’re fleeing police officers. They have to—the officer has to be able to articulate, you know, a reasonable fear of imminent harm to themself, to the other officers, or to the public.

Rosin: When we come back, more about what we can learn about how DHS is operating and the agency’s new secretary, Markwayne Mullin.

[Break]

Rosin: Now, what happened after these killings is confusing, and I think clearing it up will help us understand where immigration policy is headed. The way it was first reported is that Markwayne Mullin talked to Susan Collins, the senator of Maine, issued a policy to at least temporarily halt these traffic stops that you’re talking about. And then Trump comes out on Truth Social and seems sort of incensed by this. What did Trump write, and how do you explain what happened?

Miroff: Yeah. Well, the order of events is that Secretary Mullin talked to Susan Collins three times on Monday after the fatal shooting in Maine, and he also talked to Senator Angus King, the other senator from Maine. He had been hearing from senior ICE officials that the vehicle stops were becoming a problem and are exposing the insufficient training of these officers. So he was already hearing about these, about these worries.

And so late Monday night, an order went out from an ICE official to all the ICE supervisors around the country to halt the vehicle stops. And so the president—you know, people woke up to this Tuesday and by, you know, Wednesday morning the president sent out his his statement on Truth Social, effectively saying that they needed to continue with the traffic stops, that—kind of countermanding the order from the ICE officials and and saying that the traffic stops are a much-needed tool and that they can’t back down. And we know that he did that because throughout the day on Tuesday as, you know, hard-line—immigration hard-liners and members of the, you know, more extreme members of the MAGA coalition were telling him that it would make him look weak. And then kind of saying similar things to what they said after Minneapolis was that, you know, They’re giving in to protesters to the radical left, they can’t back down from the mass deportation campaign that Trump has promised. And, you know, clearly they broke through to him and he has reversed course. Now, you know, what we’re still seeing is that some kind of new guidance is coming to ICE officers that is going to try to introduce some additional safeguards into these vehicle stops. But it also shows that the president is very sensitive to criticism from his base about any perceived backtracking on this deportation campaign.

Rosin: Yeah, ’cause you said it calmly, but it was a lot of capitals and exclamation points. I mean, it seemed like a very aggressive statement the president was making, which doesn’t necessarily clarify the confusion, because I’m assuming that Mullin wouldn’t have issued that statement without checking with someone at the White House. So you think just afterwards there was a lot of MAGA pressure to reverse course, and it’s as simple as that?

Miroff: It was telling that on Wednesday, senior administration officials were really throwing Mullin under the bus on this one and saying that he hadn’t cleared this, you know, vehicle-stop order with the White House.

But I also spoke to people in the department who said it was—you know, that wasn’t possible, that there’s no way he would have cleared this kind of thing. He would have—in this kind of order would have gone forward without people, including, you know, Tom Homan and Stephen Miller from being aware that they were, that they were—that it was coming.

Rosin: Mullin is still in charge for the moment. Has he made any significant changes? How is he different than the previous leadership?

Miroff: Well, Mullin came in to replace Kristi Noem, who, along with her top adviser, Corey Lewandowski, ran the department in a very different way and made a lot of enemies there, generated a lot of controversy, and ultimately were removed because of the perception that she was trying to promote her own career, her own interests, and and had just become too much of a liability for the White House in a way that was undermining the president’s broader immigration-enforcement goals. You know, Mullin was brought in, I think, to stabilize the department, to bring a kind of a sunnier presence while still being somebody who was close to the president and intensely loyal to Trump and who would carry out, you know, the the campaign with a better face, so to speak.

But, you know, their goal has been to carry out the president’s mass-deportation campaign but more quietly and with less controversy. And I think that they had been relatively effective in doing that up until these shootings. But it really shows the inherent, you know, tension in what they’re trying to do. They’re trying to carry out this incredibly disruptive, intrusive, controversial, you know, deportation campaign in American cities and communities and there’s just no way it’s gonna be quiet and that people aren’t gonna push back.

Rosin: So do you have a sense that the two latest shootings could change policy at all—could shift the course of how they’re doing immigration enforcement?

Miroff: I think that after the president’s response to that order and his fear of looking weak, I think no. They’re gonna continue pressing forward. They have the money; they have the personnel. Trump has made this commitment. This is like the signature domestic-policy initiative of his second term.

So they’re going to go forward and they’re gonna try to, you know, keep a lid on this stuff politically as best they can and the question is gonna be, you know, can they pull it off? How many more of these shootings can Americans stomach?

Rosin: Well, Nick, thank you for joining us today.

Miroff: Thank you. Anytime, Hanna.

Rosin: That’s all for today’s show. This episode of Radio Atlantic was produced by Mike Pesoli. It was engineered and has original music by Rob Smierciak. Claudine Ebeid is the executive producer of Atlantic audio, and Andrea Valdez is our managing editor.

If you like what you saw here, new episodes of Radio Atlantic drop every Monday and Thursday. You can subscribe on The Atlantic’s YouTube page, on Apple or Spotify, or wherever you get your podcasts. And if you want to support this work and the work of my colleagues, you can subscribe to this publication at TheAtlantic.com/Listener. Until next time, I’m Hanna Rosin.

entries.title: The World Cup Could Not Be Americanized
entries.summary: Spain’s 1–0 win over Argentina demonstrated all of the ways that this sport is still foreign in the United States.
entries.content:
Spain’s 1–0 win over Argentina demonstrated all of the ways that this sport is still foreign in the United States.

A World Cup on North American soil ended with the most thoroughly imported, overseas-style final match possible. The foreign tourists who tried to smuggle ranch dressing through TSA; the Norwegians who scarfed down Texas brisket while doing the Viking row at Dallas barbecue joints; the garish, out-of-place halftime show in a New Jersey football stadium—all of that Americanness was overcome in the end by the classic continental display of footwork and defense in Spain’s 1–0 victory over Argentina. This event just could not be Americanized, hard as Donald Trump, the Fox network, and the Muppets tried. Men’s soccer did not become American at this World Cup—and America still does not really play men’s soccer.

Real soccer looked like this: one tap, two taps, and then flights of red Spanish jerseys surging toward the goal. La Roja played such elegant, staccato, soft-shoed ball that they seemed to have 20 men on the field instead of 11. With their quick-step precision, they completed a dazzling 789 passes. Sorry, but Americans just don’t kick it like that—not yet, anyway. Fully 89 percent of Spain’s touches, whether nosing the ball along or lashing it, went right where they intended.

But the game, almost incomprehensibly, also looked like this: Despite Spain’s brilliance, it was a scoreless tie right up until the 105th minute of extra time, when Ferran Torres laced a goal with a left-footer to the high back net. All of that nullity was thanks to the record-breaking heroics of Argentina’s goalie Damian Emiliano “Dibu” Martínez, who had 11 saves, the most recorded in a final since the World Cup began keeping stats. He launched himself sideways, again and again, to get his gloves on Spain’s ballistic leg whips. Spain completely dominated—they took 20 shots overall compared with just two for Argentina—and yet they could have lost. That’s “the football” for you. It’s a game of low scores, which can mean rough justice. A team can be clearly superior and not get a bit of what it deserves. As Argentina’s captain, Lionel Messi, reflected a couple of years ago in a TV interview, “In football you can be 10 times better than your opponent and still lose.” He added, “These things in football are difficult to explain no matter how much you analyze and study it.”

This game was likely the final World Cup appearance for Messi, the greatest player of his generation if not all time, whom Spain limited to just one shot, which was blocked. Argentina had trouble even getting the ball in Spain’s half of the field, unable to make connections against a quicker-toed, fast-closing defense that gave up just a single goal in its eight World Cup matches. Yet Messi still managed to create a sense of late drama with his hooking passes across the face of Spain’s net, conjuring sudden opportunities out of nothing.

American players just don’t seem to have that kind of threat in their shoes. Think about the difference between American soccer prodigies and a Messi, who took up the game at the age of 3 or 4 in his sprawling home city of Rosario. The son of a house cleaner and a supervisor in a metal factory, he played for a local nonprofit club that sometimes didn’t have enough practice balls to go around. His footwork is partly a function of improvised games he played by himself as a boy; as he related in an early TV interview, he would juggle oranges and tennis balls off his insteps. He could kick an orange 60 times in a row without it hitting the ground; a tennis ball, 120 times.

One of Messi’s earliest coaches, Carlo Marconi, once recalled that he baited Messi with cookies for every goal he scored. Messi had a particular passion for alfajores, caramel-flavored treats, and would score four or five goals a game to get them. He scored so often that Marconi decided to change the game: He would give Messi a double reward if he could score on a header. Marconi recalled: “So what did he do? He would start here, dribble past everyone, get to the goal, kick the ball up with his foot, and score with his head. Then he’d say to me, ‘Two.’” But Marconi also remembered that Messi would divide the spoils among his teammates, handing out alfajores to other boys. Marconi said he ended up just giving Messi cookies for free: “Here, take it. But I’m giving it to you not because you’re a good player, but because you’re a good person.”

That story almost certainly wouldn’t happen today in America, for the simple reason that the youth-soccer culture here is mainly built on a profit-driven “pay to play” system. Parents fork over heavy fees to enroll their child in local clubs that charge for training, field rentals, coaching, and travel. As a result, soccer remains a mostly suburban, middle- and upper-class sport. (Not that soccer is the only game that requires American parents to dig in their pockets: According to one study, the cost of all youth sports rose by 46 percent from 2019 to 2025.)

The cure for this problem is not more spending. The U.S. Soccer Federation spent more than $6 million in yearly salary and incentives on an illustrious European head coach, Mauricio Pochettino, yet his overhyped team was beaten, badly, the minute it faced a top-10-ranked team. Belgium’s 4–1victory over the U.S. in the round of 16 came despite intercession by the American president to have the suspension of the U.S.’s best player, Folarin Balogun, overturned after he was given a red card. “Overturn this,” the Belgium team posted on its social-media account after the game. Balogun, by the way, is really the product of British—not American—soccer. He has U.S. birthright citizenship because he was born in Brooklyn to a Nigerian mother who was merely visiting. He was reared and trained in London.

Trump’s heavy shoulder lean on FIFA regarding the red card—“I was the one who got them to do it,” he acknowledged—soured much of the goodwill that foreign fans may have had toward the U.S. as a co-host of the tournament. Unfortunately, another breach of FIFA rules and tradition came at halftime of the final and didn’t win many friends either. FIFA mandates that halftime should be no longer than 15 minutes. Soccer—real football—is a game of rhythm and fluidity, with only a brief break. There had never been a “halftime show” at a World Cup. Yet because this World Cup was in America, a halftime show there was. The verdict on the 27-minute festival of gaudiness, featuring Madonna, Justin Bieber, Shakira, and various Muppet characters, is that America showed off better when it didn’t try quite so hard. It was a relief when Spain retook the field, restoring a sense of elegance, and leaving a far more lingering imprint on the field.

entries.title: The Crisis That Novelists Can’t Handle
entries.summary: Amitav Ghosh believes that climate change requires rethinking the novel. His new work of fiction fails that standard.
entries.content:
Amitav Ghosh believes that climate change requires rethinking the novel. His new work of fiction fails that standard.

Is there any subject that fiction cannot address? From its beginnings as a form, the novel has dealt with love and hate, war and peace, courage and cowardice, boredom and catastrophe. Yet there is far more in heaven and earth than any human can convey. In the view of the writer Amitav Ghosh, one subject especially threatens to elude fiction’s comprehension. “The climate crisis,” he writes in his book-length 2016 essay, The Great Derangement, “is also a crisis of culture, and thus of the imagination.”

Across his many novels and nonfiction books, Ghosh has been attempting to correct this shortcoming. He has become a leading theorist of the relationship between global climate change and culture, arguing that the sparsity of the former in fiction owes to a “grid of literary forms and conventions” that coalesced just as “carbon in the atmosphere was rewriting the destiny of the earth.” Contemporary writing, he believes, is not up to the challenge of depicting forces so great and impersonal that they seem to actively confound attempts at storytelling.

It makes sense that Ghosh would become a writer of ecological rupture, as he is, in a sense, the product of one. At some point in the mid-1850s, the river Padma suddenly carved a new path through the village of his ancestors in modern-day Bangladesh, flooding their homes and scattering them across the Indian subcontinent. As a result, Ghosh was born in Calcutta, India, in 1956. His family’s collective memory taught him about the destructive capriciousness of the natural world, and it made him a keen observer of human migration throughout history. It also informs his skepticism of the modern realist novel.

In much of literary fiction, characters and settings are developed slowly, through the gradual accrual of discrete moments that rarely stray from the mundane and the reasonable. Yet this supposedly “realist” form, Ghosh argues, is in fact engaged in a “concealment of the real.” For many people on Earth, life is full of abrupt and irrevocable events. Homes flood; tornadoes rip up crops; fires swallow families. To be unfamiliar with these outcomes is to be the outlier, not the norm. So why, Ghosh wonders, are such rapid turnabouts so rarely present in the genre?

Ghost-Eye, his 11th and latest novel, attempts to push back on this aversion to the improbable, and thus to the catastrophic—though it fails to effectively solve the problem. In Calcutta, readers meet Varsha, a 3-year-old girl who is the youngest child of Abhay Gupta, a businessman and heir to a family that has “risen, in one generation, from modest to quite extravagant wealth.” But in September of 1969, the toddler begins to recall another life, when she lived in an impoverished island village and ate fish. Varsha has only ever lived in a mansion and has never even visited the coast, but she insists on her recollections. This causes significant dismay among her upper-caste and strictly vegetarian Marwari Hindu family, which calls in a local doctor, and then his wife, the psychologist Shoma Bose.

Their choice is fortuitous. Though Shoma is “dogmatically materialist,” with little interest in the supernatural, she has in the past conducted research on “cases of the reincarnation type”—young children who begin to vividly recall an existence not a bit like their own. She had embarked on this research only within the past few years, after she became the Bengali-language translator for an American researcher, Cathy Booth. Shoma initially considers it “unimaginable that reincarnation could be the subject of serious scientific research,” and her husband, Monty, calls Booth “the ghost-doctor.” But Shoma is quickly convinced of its plausibility by the subjects she meets while working with Booth. This shift sets up a narrative pattern in the novel: deep skepticism followed by immediate conversion.

In its opening stretch, Ghost-Eye alternates between Shoma’s 1969 consultations with the young Varsha and the experiences of Shoma’s nephew, Dinanth Datta, called Dinu by his aunt. During “the plague year of 2020,” Dinu begins to reflect on his childhood. He had often left the modest, overcrowded home of his family to spend time with Shoma and Monty at their large Art Deco estate, called Tavoy. His intimate, sensual memories capture the cacophony of city sounds, the tumult of left-wing agitation, and the vivid darkness of Calcutta’s periodic blackouts, “the like of which,” he notes, “I have never again experienced anywhere in the world.” These recollections are fond, but tinged with the knowledge of all that would soon change; even the name of his city is no longer the same.

The novel thus proceeds in two registers, at once mysterious and elegiac. In the present, Dinu is old and lonely. He begins every day by checking the increasing concentration of CO2 in the atmosphere and a running tracker of how many species went extinct during the previous day. Shoma, even older, is bed-bound with a broken hip. Dinu’s reflections are intercut with stories from their past, memories of Varsha’s therapeutic work, and stories of the migrants and post-partition refugees who lived and worked at Tavoy.

Ghost-Eye is at its best when Ghosh remains in this intimate mode, following lives that persist in the face of political and ecological disruption. Yet this is the lesser part of the novelist’s plan. During the early pandemic, Dinu begins receiving FaceTime calls from Tipu, a young gay man who is working at a nonprofit in the Sundarbans, a network of coastal mangrove islands that spread across the Ganges Delta. In the nonprofit’s archives, Tipu has found a mention of a trip that Shoma made in 1969 to the islands, and he has become convinced that this visit has some cryptic connection to a project he refuses to divulge more about.

Ghosh has written about the Sundarbans at least twice before, in 2004’s The Hungry Tide and 2019’s Gun Island; both Dinu and Tipu were introduced in the latter. Those novels involved investigations into history, and the past is clearly Ghosh’s comfort zone. Shoma’s investigation of Varsha’s reincarnation, and Dinu’s investigation of Shoma, give the first part of Ghost-Eye a satisfyingly retrospective quality. Yet Ghosh means to paint a picture not only of Dinu’s life in the 2020s, but also of the entire world, and then he aims to reach into the future. Varsha’s mysterious memories are apparently linked to a much larger set of crises, and in order to make sense of these connections, both Dinu and Ghosh will have to dispense with the material and tread into the metaphysical.

To say that Ghost-Eye is a novel of disaster would be underselling it. Storms smash into cities, disease shuts down whole societies, and millions of humans migrate across the globe. The scale of these events transcends the limits of any individual experience—making it difficult for the typical realistic novel to contain them, as Ghosh has argued.

At the same time, natural disasters, fed and intensified by man-made climate change, are entering the realm of the familiar. In the decade since Ghosh published The Great Derangement, a few novelists have attempted to make sense of these disruptions in fiction. In Jenny Offill’s Weather, climate change intrudes on its narrator’s mundane day-to-day thoughts, a nagging anxiety that never quite boils over. Lydia Kiesling’s Mobility attempts something at once more traditional and more ambitious, exploring how a young woman tries to disentangle herself from the oil industry’s poisonous tendrils. Both do a fine job of capturing how it feels to live through an age of human-engineered hazards. Yet these novels, alongside other recent works by Richard Flanagan and Alexandra Kleeman, struggle to convey what Ghosh describes in The Great Derangement as the “scalar” dimensions of the Anthropocene, an age defined by “forces of unthinkable magnitude” that both dwarf and discount any one perspective.

As it goes on, Ghost-Eye becomes a quest to recover ancient, repressed forces—the supernatural powers rejected by modernity, yet still alive in the spiritualism infusing the lives of most people on the planet. In the course of her investigation, Shoma begins to suspect that Varsha is clairvoyant. The pair’s mysterious interactions in the early chapters give way to a series of parables of doubt and belief, in which a rational character is confronted by an irrational event—and immediately convinced of its absolute truth. These conversions are not grounded in the characters that Ghosh has sketched, but rather in the plot he needs them to advance. Character gives way to contrivance, and Ghost-Eye collapses under the strain.

The trouble, once again, comes down to questions of scale. As Ghosh himself has noted, the “realistic” novel is loath to make space for unlikely events—yet his solution to this problem is at odds with his own skill set. In the end, Ghosh is a very traditional novelist, adept at sketching scenes and enlivening characters with well-placed detail. But as his novel goes on, nighttime scenes and vivid memories are replaced by exchanges of purely expository dialogue, which seeks to explain to the reader, point by point, the book’s ghostly developments. This approach substitutes information for sensation, and reduces both human and supra-human experiences to the level of data. (This reaches its absolute nadir in Tipu’s video calls, where this gay Gen Zer is reduced to prompting Dinu with humiliating lines such as “Okay, Pops, since you’re trippin’ on me, I’ll spill the tea.”)

Rather than creating “unbearably intimate connections,” as Ghosh has called them, between the individual and planetary scales of climate change, these tricks merely reveal the flimsiness of his narrative. The novel’s climax—a sequence defined by reality-shattering magical spectacle—is narrated by one character to another over FaceTime, a dreadful decision that deflates the entire story.

Perhaps it isn’t so much the realist novel that resists catastrophe, but Ghosh’s narrow conception of the form. In The Great Derangement, he slams the avant-garde movements of the 20th century, which he believes unconsciously undermined collective narration by exalting the experience of the individual. This is a bold claim to make about modernism as a whole. Among the diverse international literatures grouped together under that heading, there are dozens of writers who reinvented literature to cope with ruptures of their own. In novels such as Thousand Cranes and The Master of Go, Yasunari Kawabata wedded stream-of-consciousness to classical forms of ritual and performance, modeling rapid Japanese westernization through stories that overlay the ancient and the modern. Juan Rulfo infused his novel Pedro Páramo with half a century of political and economic violence, envisioning rural Mexico as a mythic space where past and present overlap. In his classic work The Obscene Bird of Night, the Chilean writer José Donoso created a literary mirror for a South America in crisis; his mutable story’s transformations—of subject, temporality, point of view—replicate the developments that were roiling his home country.

These writers did not merely replicate modernity; they responded to it, transforming the novel to address a world in constant and often cataclysmic flux. Artistic innovations are often driven by crises, after all, and the novel is no different. Ghosh is right to argue that naturalistic narratives are not up to the urgent task of depicting our unstable moment. But he is wrong in thinking that no recent counterexamples exist. Perhaps the problem of art in the time of climate change is not that its subjects are too immense to be grasped. We might be dealing instead with a lack of artistic imagination, an inability or an unwillingness to adapt the form of the novel to suit the subject. In this respect, Ghost-Eye is more the norm than the exception. But where Ghosh has failed, others might succeed.
